hook: Small pox inoculation technique went from Asia to Europe. What was it called in India?
explanation: Quick read: Covid tikah is a carryover from small pox language._x000D_ _x000D_ Note: Long before the discovery of vaccination in the late 18th century, a practice known as inoculation or variolation offered some protection against the dreaded smallpox. While this technique gained attention in Europe only in the 18th century, it had been widely practiced in India, China, and the Caucasus for centuries earlier._x000D_ _x000D_ In India, the method, called "tikah" by the natives, involved introducing pus from a mature smallpox pustule into a healthy individual, typically through a prick on the forehead. This mild exposure triggered a controlled infection, granting partial immunity to future outbreaks._x000D_ _x000D_ Historical records like an East India Company report from 1731 confirm this practice's longevity. It states that "inoculation... has been known in the kingdom of Bengal about 150 years," placing its origins in India well before the 18th century. Moreover, another report from 1767 describes specialized Brahmin communities traveling in groups to perform inoculations during outbreaks._x000D_ _x000D_ British also condescendingly observed that ignorance and barbarism can also produce valuable improvements. Under British rule, these techniques gradually died out._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: “Indian Science & Technology in the Eighteenth Century”, Dharampal._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: The Wikimedia image is of anti cholera inoculation camp at Kolkata in 1894.

hook: In which year was Ahilyabai born?
explanation: Ahilyabai was born on May 31, 1725. Her father was a Patil of village Choundi. She had five brothers. Several monuments have been built at Choundi, both by Ahilyabai and by others. There is a ghat in the river and a temple in the village has been built called Ahileshwar Mahadev. A moorthy of Ahilyabai stands worshipping Shiva.

hook: Malhar Rao was instrumental in grooming Ahilyabai into a Queen. What was Malhar Rao to Ahilyabai?
explanation: Malhar Rao Holkar had the biggest influence on Ahilyabai and was her mentor too. He was the one who spotted the young Ahilyabai and brought her to Indore as his daughter-in-law. He would make Ahilyabai his successor in royal matters, even though she was never formally the Subedar. He taught her statecraft and integrity. He trained Ahilyabai in horse riding and archery along with other Sons he had sired. Malhar Rao himself was from a shepherd family. He was enrolled in the army and gathered fame with his fighting capabilities.

hook: At what age was Ahilyabai married?
explanation: Ahilyabai was married to Khande Rao when she was eight. She moved into Malharao's house in Indore. Malhar Rao saw great potential in her and trained her in accounting, finance, statecraft, history, horse riding etc.

hook: Who succeeded Ahilyabai to carry on Holkar tradition?
explanation: None of the male members of Ahilyabai's family survived her. Her adopted son Tukoji and his sons survived her.

hook: How many Peshwas did Ahilyabai witness in her life?
explanation: Ahilyabai lived through the glorious era of Bajirao Peshwa. She later on witnessed the reign of Nanasaheb, Madhavrao and Narayanrao. She died during the reign of Swai Madhavrao Peshwa. Throughout her life, she remained a staunch loyalist of the Peshwas and rebuked attempts by rebels and British to defect.

hook: Where did Malhar Rao establish his rule?
explanation: Malhar Rao is considered as the founder of modern Indore. Indore was a small hamlet before he developed it. Malhar Rao made Indore his capital and built a principality on the banks of the river Kanh. He offered many incentives and facilities to traders, businessmen and artisans to migrate to Indore. He ordered the local Kanungo to populate the Kasba of Indore. He provided privileges to Sowkars and shokeepers from Ujjain.

hook: Where did Ahilyabai rule from?
explanation: After the death of her husband and son, Ahilyabai was drawn more and more towards spirituality. Yet, she was also conscious of the duty she had as Rajmata. She found peace on the banks of Narmada. It could serve as a capital and also satisfy her spiritual urges. She founded a new capital at Maheshwar i 1767 and spent 28 years of her life in Maheshwar. Her father in law Malhar Rao had already issued a warrant of protection in 1745 to the would be residents of Maheshwar with grants of land for houses for squires, carpenters, weavers and labour. There are more than sixty temples in Maheshwar. Maheshwar was known as Mahishmati in ancient India.

hook: Which produce did Ahilaybai promote in Maheshwar that it is still famous for?
explanation: Ahilyabai invited skilled weavers from all parts of India to settle down in Maheshwar. She helped them financially to establish a business. She also bought the sarees and textiles they produced. She invited Sakharam Ghongde, an expert in silk textiles to Indore. Within four years, more than 100 weavers were making Maheshwari sarees and dhotis. Because of the encouragement given by Ahilyabai, quality sarees and clothes began to be produced in Maheshwar. Maheshwar was also known for vessel making and Maheswari Silver vessels became a speciality.

hook: Which is a famous Jyotirlinga where Ahilyabai rebuilt a destroyed temple. A moorthy of Ahilyabai has recently been installed in the renovated complex now.
explanation: Ahilyabai is famous for the reconstruction of the Kashi Vishwanath temple, slightly away from the temple Aurangzeb destroyed. In 1785, she built this 51 feet high temple. She also renovated a ghat in varanasi. Her contributions to Varanasi are extensive. She built the Manikarnikaghat and a female only ghat at Manikarnika. She built 6 private temples, and Tarkeshwar temple. She built 9 private bungalows. She also established Brahmapuri as a colony of Brahmins.

hook: At what age did Ahilyabai pass away?
explanation: Ahilyabai lived upto the age of 70 in her simple palace built like a hermitage in Maheshwar on the banks of Narmada. Santaji Holkar, the brother of Tukoji was at her side. Gifts and donations for the welfare of the passing soul were distributed. Her last moments were of quiet and calm. Ahilyabai's favourite cow which she saw every morning also died alongwith her. A beautiful Chattri has been built at the spot of her cremation. John Malcom says of her life "It appears most extra-ordinary, how she had the mental powers to go through the labours she imposed on herself, from the age of 38 till death."

hook: Ahilyabai's husband KhandeRao was not inclined towards hardwork and valour. How did he die?
explanation: KhandeRao was not interested in the Kingdom, weapons, governance or education. He spent his time in drugs, alochol and in the company of women. Ahilyabai tried reforming him. For a brief while Khande Rao appeared like a changed man and started accompanying his father in expeditions. Ahilyabai would learn that his primary motive was loot and not expansion fo the kingdom and that in the battlefield too, he was involved in excesses. Khande Rao was sent to quell the rebellion of Suraj Mal. Ahilyabai also joined the battle camp after sometime and was overseeing the medical and logistics facilities. During this siege Khande Rao was killed by bullet wound.

hook: Ahilyabai broke the existing custom of queens committing Sati when their husband died. Who convinced her not to commit Sati?
explanation: Ahilyabai was preparing herself to commit Sati alongwith the funeral of KhandeRao. However Malhar Rao convinced her not to commit Sati. He asked her to take responsibility for the kingdom as there was no other male heir. Malhar Rao had always seen the future of the kingdom in the hands of Ahilyabai. She relented, but wore white saree and gave up jewellery and decorations for the rest of her life.

hook: Mahar Rao was constantly in war expeditions around the country. Who ran the State in his absence?
explanation: Malhar Rao was constantly in war expeditions at the service of Peshwa. Therefore the day to day administration of the State was in Ahilyabai's hands. He had full faith in her administrative and political abilities. There was constant correspondence between the two even when Malhar Rao was away. He would give instructions for supply of men, money and material to the warfront which Ahilyabai would fulfil. He would also provide instructions of production of ammunition and artillery. He would also leave instructions on administrative aspects and governance.

hook: Maley Rao, son of Ahilyabai, had a short reign as the Sardar of Malwa. How long was his reign?
explanation: As a child Maley Rao cultivated destructive habits. He was fond of playing cruel practical games on visitors and subjects. He was also very arrogant. Maley Rao had even killed the lover of his favourite consort Heera. Maley Rao had also been introduced into drugs and alcohol by Khande Rao when he was alive. Even though Maley Rao was made Sardar, the real power was in the hands of Ahilyabai. A combination of bad habits and poor lifestyle deteriorated the health of Maley Rao. He died at the age of 22, just nine months after the death of Malhar Rao.

hook: Ahilyabai was a also a warrior. She had fought in a battle against an unlikely enemy. Who was it?
explanation: After Ahilyabai’s son died, Raghoba eyed her kingdom. Ahilyabai displayed her willingness to fight him and this dissuaded Raghoba. Ahilyabai’s battlefield victory was against Rajputs. Malhar Rao had mediated in a war against the brotherly conflict in the Jaipur Kingdom. He received the Rampur area as thanksgiving which was not liked by the Chandravats of Rampur. They rose in revolt in 1771. Ahilyabai herself took part in the battle and crushed them. Their revolt in 1783 was also crushed. Eventually, sensing weakness in Holkar kingdom, the Chandravats attacked Marathas again and the local Maratha commander fled. An infuriated Ahilyabai asked Sharief Bhai to lead a decisive battle against the Chandravats. She herself was in the battlefield arranging logistics and medical facilities. The Chandravats were defeated by the Martahas. On the order of Ahilyabai, the Chandravat rebel Saubhag Singh was tied to a canon and blown up. The Peshwa and Nana Fadnavis recognised Ahilyabai's valour in the battlefield. Till then Ahilyabai was known for her piety and governance. The crushing of the Rajput revolt made her a valiant leader as well.

hook: While she personally avoided Sati, her daughter and daughter-in-laws committed Sati. What measure did Ahilyabai take to protect widows?
explanation: Ahilyabai was against the custom of Sati but was not able to overcome conservatives who supported the practice. However she ensured that no woman was forced to commit Sati against her free will. She gave full protection to the right of widows. They had complete rights to the property of their dead husbands. This ensured that their future is protected if they decide not to commit Sati. She also gave permission to widows to adopt a Son without needing State permission or going to pay a tax to the State. These steps greatly reduced the instances of Sati in the society.

hook: Ahilyabai's handling of Bhil tribals demonstrated another quality of hers. What was it?
explanation: Bhil tribals dominated the forest area of her State. They used to rob the travellers and because of this trade had got affected. Ahlyabhai initially tried diplomacy and negotiations with Bhils which failed. She then launched a firm military action against them. Many bhils were imprisoned and several were hanged to death. The firm action made Bhils seek pardon from Ahilyabai. She provided the Bhils with arable land and necessary equipment to support them to shift to farming.

hook: Ahilyabai's reign is known for which of the following?
explanation: Ahilyabai was renowned for all aspects of governance. She separated personal funds from the State funds. She was efficient in the collection of taxes and in depositing the share of Peshwa from the treasury and from the war loots. The justice system that she administered was quick and unbiassed. It took into consideration emotional aspects. She respected the practicality and quick justice provided by institutions like the Panchayats. Her charitable work is of course renowned and all major pilgrimage places of India have seen the contributions of Ahilyabai.

hook: What were the struggles of life of Ahilyabai?
explanation: As a Rajmata Ahilyabai was unparallelled. The official communication records; and the accounts of historians and rival rulers acknowledge the prowess of Ahilyabai in governance, diplomacy, politics, justice and charity. Nana Fadnavis, Jadunath Sircar, Kavi Moropant, John Malcom and Vinobha Bhave all have recorded their appreciation of the rule of Ahilyabai. However, her personal life was unfortunate. Her husband and son fell into wasteful activities and died young. She lost her grandson and son-in-law also at a young age. She adopted Tukoji and conferred Subedarship on him but fell out with him in latter days. She herself acknowledged that she was unable to groom a heir. Finally, while she herself avoided Sati due to the beseeching of her father-in-law, she was unable to stop her daughters-in-law and her daughter from committing Sati.

hook: Who maintains the charitable properties created by Ahilyabai Holkar?
explanation: The Government of Madhya Pradesh created a Trust called The Khasgi (Devi Ahilyabai Holkar Charities) Trust in 1962. The public properties built by Ahilyabai with private funds were transferred to the Trust which would be responsible for maintaining them. However a series of unauthorised sale of Trust properties took place. In response to a complaint filed by Sumitra Mahajan, who was the MP from Indore, in 2012, the Govt of Madhya Pradesh took over the Trust properties. This was challenged by the Trust in courts. In 2010 the Supreme Court held that the Trust cannot sell the properties without proper approval from the Registrar and the Trust will have to be registered as a Public Trust. Several properties had already been sold by 2012 and the Trust continues to be in controversy regarding these sales. Source: SC Judgement on SLP (CIVIL) No. 12133 of 2020

hook: Harkuaai was a compaion to Ahilyabai till her death.What was she in the Holkar family?
explanation: Harkuaai was the unwedded wife of Malhar Rao. She had married his sword (hence Khandarani) as per custom and was therefore referred to as Mahalarrao's wife. She was just four years older to Ahilyabai and was a constant companion to her.

hook: What is the name of the only son of Ahilyabai?
explanation: Maleyrao was the only son of Ahilyabai, but was lost to wasteful habits.

hook: What was the name of Ahilyabai’s husband?
explanation: Ahilyabai was brought into the Holkar family as the wife of Khanderao, the son of Malhar Rao. However, Khanderao had wasteful habits, was a drunkard and drug addict. He also had several concubines. Though Ahilyabai had two children, her married life was a disappointment and she was always wistful that she never felt what love is.

hook: Tukoji is a key figure in the Holkar history. What was he to Ahilyabai?
explanation: Ahilyabai adopted Tukoji after the death of her husband and son. Tukoji was Malhar Rao’s nephew and was also considered as his son. Ahilyabai adopted him and made him Subedhar. They had a very uneasy relationship. Tukoji would have lavish personal expenses and would fail to credit the share of Peshwas from battle loots.

hook: Yashwant Phanse may have become the Subedhar but for his unfortunate early death. Who was he to Ahilyabai?
explanation: Yashwant Phanse was married to the only daughter of Ahilyabai. He was courageous and may have become the Subedhar as Ahilyabai's son had also died. Unfortunately, Cholera took Yashawant's life at a very young age. This devastated Ahilyabai, who would also lose her daughter Mukta to the custom of Sati.

hook: Nathobha who died due to tuberculosis was another close relative of Ahilyabai. What was he?
explanation: Nathoba was the son of Ahilyabai’s daughter Mukta and Yashwant. He was continuously ill and was a weak child. He had tuberculosis died in the year1790, at a very young age of 23; and without children. Both his wives committed Sati with him.

hook: Gangoba Tatya was a trusted aide of Ahilyabai would eventually switch sides. What was his role?
explanation: Gangoba was the accountant for the Holkar dynasty. He was very much impressed by the integrity and welfare orientation of Ahilyabai. Whenever the male members wanted to access the official wealth for personal expenses, Ahilyabai will instruct Gangoba not to accept the request.

hook: Who was appointed as the Sardar of Malwa after the death of Malhar Rao?
explanation: Malhar Rao waged expeditions with renewed vigour after the Maratha defeat at the Battle of Panipat. He spent five years raiding northern India. Malhar received an injury in the battle of Mangrol. He died at Alampur in Madhya Pradesh in 1766. Khande Rao had of course died before. Tukoji Holkar and Maley Rao were with Malhar Rao when he died. Malhar appointed Maley Rao as his successor and Tukoji Holkar as Maley Rao's guardian.

hook: After the death of Ahilyabai's son, which Maratha leader tried to overthrow Holkar's rule in Malwa?
explanation: The Diwan appointed by Malhar Rao, Gangadhar Pant Chadrachur, advised Ahilyabai to adopt a son who will become Sardar. However, Ahilyabai realized it would mean the real power would shift to the officials, and refused. Gangadhar Pant wrote to Raghunath Rao Peshwa advising him to invade and conquer the State. Ahilyabai then sought the help of Scindias, Gaikwad and Bhonsales. All of them were loyal to the legacy of Malhar Rao and agreed to support her. Ahilyabai also obtained approval of Madhava Rao Peshwa to defend her State. She also raised an a compact woman army. With the odds staked against him, Raghunath Rao conceded defeat, left his army behind and visited Ahilyabai to offer his condolences for the death of her son. With this move, Ahilyabai had cemented her position as Rajmata of Indore. Later on, she would also snub Raghunath Rao's efforts to usurp the Peshwa throne after the murder of Narayanrao Peshwa; and also warned him strictly against taking British support. On the death of Raghunath Rao, Ahilyabai would remark that an eclipse had passed over and that a traitor is no more.

hook: Yashwant Rao Phanse overcame highway bandits in the Malwa state. How was he recognised for this achievement?
explanation: Law and order had deteriorated in the kingdom after the Panipat war. Bhil tribals were causing trouble. Tribal thieves and robbers were rampant robbing travellers. In an open court room, Ahilyabai declared that she will marry her daughter Mukta to the person who controls the bandits. This was in total disregard to the then custom of royalty, caste, ancestry etc. Yashwant Rao Phanse took up the challenge on the condition that he be provided with resources and manpower.

hook: Which city in Maharashtra has been renamed as Ahilyanagar?
explanation: Ahilyabai was born in Chondi (also Chauri) village in Maharashtra. It is about 85 kms from erstwhile Ahmednagar which was renamed as Ahilyanagar in 2024.

hook: Which city in India has an airport named after Ahilyabai?
explanation: The Indore airport is named after Ahilyabai. The Bhopal airport is named after the legendary Raja Bhoj. The Gwalior airport is named after Rajmata Vijayaraje Scindia. Ujjain does not have an airport yet.

hook: Which former Lok Sabha speaker wrote a book on Ahilyabai?
explanation: Sumitra Mahajan is a Maharashtrian born in Chiplun. She married into the Mahajan family of Indore. She completed her BA and MA from the Indore University which is now named as Ahilyabai university. She was the Deputy Mayor of Indore Municpal Corporation. She was elected as an MP from the Indore constituency. She wrote a book named "Matoshree" on the life of Ahilyabai.

hook: Which aspect of Hinduism was revived by the marathas in general, and Ahilyabai in particular.
explanation: The marathas and Ahilyabai supported the Hindu revival in all aspects. However Ahilyabai's contribution to the revival of pilgrim centres stands out. Foreign conquest of about ten centuries had levelled many pilgrimage places of India. The Marathas were instrumental in the reconstruction of the Hindu society and its institutions. Ahilyabai was the best exponent of it. With her charity work, she ensured that several temples were reconstructed, ghats were built in tirthas, choultries were built for accommodation of pilgrims and water facilities were provided. About her charity, John Malcom says, "hours snatched from the affairs of the State were all given to acts of devotion and charity - and a deep sense of religion appears to have strengthened her mind in the performance of worldly duties."

hook: Maheshwar, the capital founded by Ahilyabai, was also host to a famous religious debate of India. Who participated in the debate?
explanation: The famous debate between Adi Shankaracharya and Mandan Mishra took place at Mahishmati. The place is also referred to in Meghdoot of Kalidasa. Mahishmati is also referred to in the Itihaasaas and puranas.

hook: Ahilyabai was known for unparallelled construction activity in pilgrimage centres. How were these funded?
explanation: A speciality of Holkar family was that they separated personal expenses from the public funds. The construction activities were funded out of the personal funds received by the Holkar family. Ahilyabai's mother in law had received a few villages for her maintenance from the Peshwa. The income from this and other gifts received by the queen constituted the personal fund. It is believed that this grant was at the request of Malhar Rao who was continuing the custom from his Dhangar society. Four annas out of the earnings of the day was given to the wife. Malhar Rao also personally requested for a grant from Peshwa to his wife similar to this practice. Ahilyabai inherited this fund of about sixteen crores at that time after her mother-in-law's death. A letter from Peshwa confirms this inheritance. After she lost her son Male Rao, she decided to use the personal fund for charity. All the construction activity of Ahilyabai were funded out of this.

hook: What was the focus of the construction activity of Ahilyabai?
explanation: When Malhar Rao was alive, Ahilyabai had visited several pilgrimage places. She was conscious of the expenses that pilgrims incurred and the hardships they had to face. She decided to spend her personal wealth on renovation of pilgrimage facilities. Every major pilgrimage center in India has the stamp of Ahilyabai's generosity and public work. There are facilities in all corners of India - Kedarnath, Badrinath, Rameswaram, Pandharpur, Somnath and Puri. To make sure that the facilities are well maintained, she also created permanent financial arrangements for their upkeep.

hook: Apart from Varanasi which she is famous for, Ahilyabai also rebuilt another Jyotirlinga. Where is this?
explanation: Ahilyabai also rebuilt the Somnath temple that was destroyed by Ghazni Mahmud. This is slightly away from the original Somnath temple that was destroyed. Even almost 75 years after the reconstruction of the grand Somnath temple at the original spot, the temple that Ahilyabai built is worshipped regularly. There is a constant stream of pilgrims to the Ahilyabai temple at Somnath.

hook: Which group of tirthas were choosen by Ahilyabai for charitable and infrastructure works?
explanation: Ahilyabai executed development works in all these group of tirthas. Eight Jyotirlingas have records of the contribution of Ahilyabai. In another four, the contributions of Holkars are recorded but is not clear which ruler made them. She made contributions in all 7 Puris (moksha cities) of Ayodhya, Mathura, Dwarka, Haridwar, Benares, Kanchi and Ujjain. She also made contribution to the Char Dhams of Puri, Dwarka, rameswaram and Badrinath.

hook: Ahilyabai arranged to send Ganga Jal to several temples. How many temples receive Ganga Jal sent by Ahilyabai even now?
explanation: Ahilyabai made permanent arrangements to send Ganga Jal from Gangotri to forty temples every year on the day of Shivaratri. These include the Jyotirlingas, Pashupatinath, Gokarna and Pandharpur. This tradition continues and the descendants of Brahmins appointed by Ahilyabai still carryout this activity.

hook: Water management was one of the focus of Ahilyabai. Where in Maharashtra will you find Ahilyabai lake spread over 18 acres?
explanation: The Ahilyabai lake is in Jejuri. It was built by Ahilyabai in 1770. She dedicated it to her father-in-law and mother-in-law. It is a stone structure with strong technical design to ensure water pressure and flow. It stands as a model for water management and is spread over 18 acres. It draws water from several springs in the mountains.

hook: Malhar Rao made a few fatal errors which proved costly for the maratha empire. What was his first error which made him arrive late in Delhi for a campaign against Abdali in 1957?
explanation: Antaji Mankeshwar had been defending Delhi against Abdali. He urgently sought reinforcements. Malhar Rao was despatched to support Delhi but he took a detour through Rajputana to collect taxes. By the time Malhar Rao reached Agra, Abdali had been successful in looting Delhi. The marathas eventually won and captured Najieeb Khan who was supporting Abdali.

hook: What is the second mistake of Malhar Rao which would prove costly for Marathas?
explanation: Najib Khan had been installed as a de facto controller of Delhi by Abdali. The Maratha laid a siege on Delhi and eventually captured Najib Ali. Through intermediaries Najib convinced Malhar Rao and offered money in exchange of his freedom. Malhar Rao supported this deal even though the maratha army was against it. Najib was freed but would again ally with Abdalai in the Panipat war. Malhar Rao suffered shame because of these errors. AhilyaBai was instrumental in consoling him and helping him in regaining maratha territories in the north.

hook: Who eventually succeeded to the throne of Malwa after the death of Ahilyabai?
explanation: Yashwant Holkar was the son of Radhi, the concubine of Tukoji. By the time Ahilyabai died, Tukoji had also become old. Quarrels started among his sons for succession. This was made worse by the palace struggles within the maratha kingdom itself. Eventually, Yashwant rao succeeded.

hook: Who carried on the warrior legacy of Malhar Rao after his death?
explanation: After the death of Malhar Rao, Ahilyabai’s son Maley Rao reigned for a short period. Tukoji Rao became Subedar after Maley Rao’s death. While he was the subedar, real power was in the hands of Ahilyabai. Tukoji took on the responsibility of war expeditions for the Peshwa. He would travel north and south. He was engaged in the campaign against Tippu Sultan. His support was instrumental in controlling the revolt of Raghoba.

hook: What is the royal palace of Holkars in Indore called?
explanation: Rajwada palace is an impressive seven storey structure in Indore. Malhar Rao started the construction of the palace in 1747 and it was completed in 1766. It was renovated between 1811 and 1833. A fire and riots in 1984 damaged the palace. Two floors also caved in recently. The palace was opened for visitors again in 2023 after a six year restoration work.

hook: Till when did the Holkar dynasty last?
explanation: The Holkar dynasty continued till the independence of India. Yashwant Rao Holkar II was the last King and he acceded to the Union of India. On 16th June 1948, the princely State of Indore merged into Madhya Bharat.

hook: The friendship of Holkar dynasty with another Maratha confederacy was legendary. Which is this dynasty?
explanation: The Scindias ruled from Ujjain and Gwalior. The Scindia - Holkar friendship was legendary and it was instrumental in establishing maratha supremacy in northern India. Both Malhar Rao and Ahilyabai steadfastly tried to maintain the friendship. There were occasional incidents of mistrust and internal fighting, but the friendship endured throughout the reign of Malhar Rao and Ahilyabai.

hook: Rao Nandlal had a crucial role to play in the rise of Holkars. What was he?
explanation: Rao Nandlal was the revenue collector for the northern region for Mughals upto the river Narmada. He stayed in Indore. He was dejected by the cruel rule of Daya Bahadur, the subedar of Mughal emperor Muhammad Shah. Therefore, when Peshwa Bajirao was looking to expand the maratha empire, Rao Nandlal invited him to attack Malwa and establish Hindavi self rule. Peshwa Bajirao sensed an opportunity and sent Malhar Rao Holkar with a large army to attach Malwa. With the co-operation of Rao Nandlal, Malwa was won over by the marathas.

hook: Who did Ahilyabai appoint as Tukoji's successor shortly before her death?
explanation: The differences between Tukoji and Ahilyabai kept growing. Tukoji had not been contributing to the treasury and was retaining war loot with himself. Due to growing differences with Ahilyabai, Tukoji wished to give up the Subedarship. He requested that his son Malhar Rao be made Subedar. Ahilyabai refused as she considered him lacking in integrity. She appointed his other Son Kashi Rao as Subedar and his nephew Bapu Holkar as the Commander-in-Chief. The formal appointment by Peshwa was not completed due to the sudden demise of Mahadji Shinde in Pune.

hook: In which language was the story of Rama first told outside India?
explanation: The early Buddhist Jataka versions of Ramayana are the books ‘Anamakam Jatakam’ and ‘Dashrath Kathanam’. These were translated into Chinese in the 3rd-5th cent CE. As Buddhism expanded to the north of India, these Jataka stories went with it. Kang Seng Hui rendered the Jataka form of the Ramayana into Chinese in AD 251. In AD 472, the Nidana of Dasaratha was translated from a lost Sanskrit text by Kekaya. Source: Lokesh Chandra, Ramayana - The Epic of Asia Kamil Bulke - Many Ramayanas - Ramakatha. Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: The first Ramayana work in Sri Lanka was composed in the 6th Century, but not in Sinhalese. Which language was it in?
explanation: Sanskrit. Due to Buddhist influence, Pali, and alongwith it Sanskrit, heavily influenced Sinhalese vocabulary._x000D_ _x000D_ In the sixth century, a Sinhalese poet Kumaradasa composed Janakitharana. This is the earliest Sankrit work of Sri Lanka. Indian and Sinhalese writers note that there was a strong bond friendship between Kumaradasa and the legendary Indian poet Kalidasa. Janakitharana was later translated into Sinhalese in the 12th century._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Lokesh Chandra, Ramayana - The Epic of Asia_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Ramayana Kakwin’ is the popular Ramayana from which country? Like India it has many versions of the epic and a tradition of Ramlila.
explanation: Ramayana Kakwin is the most ancient version of Indonesian Ramayana dating back to the 10th cent CE. Like India, there are many literary works in Indonesia exploring less well known episodes of Ramayana. Sumansantak Kakwin is the story of Rama’s grandparents Aja & Indumati and the birth of Dashratha. Arjunvijaya is the story of Ravana’s defeat by Arjuna Sahasrabahu. The Ramlila held in Indonesia is based on relatively modern versions that developed later. These are quite different from the original Ramayana Kakwin or Valmiki. Source: Kamil Bulke - Many Ramayanas - Ramakatha. Picture Credit: Ramanujam SR

hook: Which famous 9th cent CE temple in Indonesia has the main events of Ramayana sculpted on its walls?
explanation: Prambanan(Parambrahm in Sanskrit) is a grand temple complex in Yogyakarta in Java, Indonesia. A full scale Ramayana is sculpted on its walls. They are distinctively Indonesia and are from the versions of Kawi Ramayana. The Prambanan temple looks to be dedicated to the Trimurti (Brahma, Vishnu, Mahesh). It was dedicated solely to Shiva originally and the architecture is made to resemble the abode of Shiva. Another 14th cent CE Shiva temple in East Java at Panataran also has similar Ramayana reliefs on its wall. Angkor Vat in Cambodia, now a Buddhist shrine but originally dedicated to Vishnu, also has battle scenes from the epic on its walls which are considered masterpieces. Source: Lokesh Chandra, Ramayana - The Epic of Asia Kamil Bulke - Many Ramayanas - Ramakatha. Picture Credit: Ramanujam SR

hook: Which biblical character does Ravana pray to in the Malayan Ramayana?
explanation: Islam became the main religion of Malaya between 13th -15th cent CE and that left its imprint on the epic. The first manuscript of ‘Hikayat Seri Ram’ dates to 1633 CE. Ravana prays to Prophet Adam who gets him the kingship of the four lokas. Ravana marries a princess in each loka and the respective sons born rule that loka on his behalf – Indrajit rules Devaloka, Mahiravana rules Pataal etc. The stories of the birth of Rama & Sita, their marriage and exile, Sita’s abduction, Lakshmana’s encounter with Shurpanakha and the post war events, are all widely different from Valmiki. After the war, Vibhishana marries Rama’s sister Kikavi, a name not present in Indian versions. After a second exile, Sita reconciles with Rama in the end and their sons Luv & Kusha marry rakshasa princesses. Strangely, only the description of Rama Ravana war follows Valmiki. Source: Kamil Bulke - Many Ramayanas - Ramakatha. Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: A Vietnamese inscription (in a 7th cent CE temple) shows an important Ramayana character as a Vishnu avatara. Which character is this?
explanation: Indian traders were visiting Indo China since 1st cent CE. After the Chinse Han empire receded from Champa (present day Vietnam), the traders became a major influence. Ramayana gained popularity and its author Valmiki was considered an incarnation of Vishnu. An image of Valmiki and an inscription have been found in a Champa temple belonging to the period of King Prakashadharma (7th cent CE in). The inscription extols Valmiki as an avatara of Vishnu. Source: Kamala Ratnam, Ramayana in Laos Kamil Bulke - Many Ramayanas - Ramakatha. Picture Credit: Ravana Lifting Kailasa. Danang Museum, Vietnam

hook: ‘Rama Kerti’ (Reamker) describes Rama both as an avatara of Vishnu and a Bodhisatva. Which small Buddhist country does it belong to?
explanation: Indian traders and the Khmer people of Cambodia together established many kingdoms from 1st cent CE onwards. Ramakerti is exquisite Khmer literature but the existing manuscripts are incomplete and the earliest dates to 17th cent CE. It follows Valmiki a great deal though the Malayan Seri Ram has also influenced it. One unique aspect of Ramakerti is that the bow that Rama breaks to marry Sita is not Shiva’s. It is made by her father Janaka from ‘Indrajala’. Indra’s net is a concept from Atharva Veda but used by Buddhists to describe the nature of the world – each part is similar to the whole. Source: Kamil Bulke - Many Ramayanas - Ramakatha. Picture Credit: Marcin Konsek, Wikimedia Commons, Paintings of Reamker from Royal Palace, Phnom Penh.

hook: In the Thai Ramayana, Hanuman is shown with an unusual characteristic. What is it?
explanation: In the Thai ‘Ramakien’, Hanuman is not the austere bachelor worshipped in India. He is a hero who has numerous women falling in love with him, including Ravana’s wife, Mandodari. Ramakien has many sources – Valmiki, the Malayan Seri Ram and the Khmer Ramakerti. It is set entirely in Shyam (Thailand). Some completely new stories are there. Brahma mediates between Rama & Ravana and orders Sita’s release which is declined by Ravana, leading to him being cursed. Post war, Vibhishana marries Mandodari and rules Lanka but a surviving son of Ravana revolts. Rama despatches Bharata & Shatrughana to quell the revolt and restore Vibhishana to the Lankan throne. Source: Kamil Bulke - Many Ramayanas - Ramakatha. Picture Credit: Jpatokal, Wikimedia Commons, Scene from Ramakien on a mural at Temple of Emerald Buddha, Bangkok.

hook: Ayuthya, a capital named after Ayodhya, was sacked by Burmese in the 18th century. The victors learnt Ramayana from the prisoners. Where is Ayuthya today?
explanation: The kingdom of Burma (now Myanmar) sacked the Thai capital Ayuthya in 1767 CE. The Thai prisoners of war were taken to Burma where they enacted the Ramayana. The Burmese were captivated and by 1800 CE, the Burmese version of Ramayana ‘Rama Yagan’, also called Yamayana, was composed. It is considered a milestone in Burmese literature. A Burmese version of Ram Lila called ‘Yama-pwe’ emerged which tells the story of Yama (Rama) and Thida (Sita). The performers wear elaborate masks which are also worshipped. Sita’s abduction is the most performed scene. The magical deer that distracts Rama to enable the abduction is Ravana’s sister Gambi (Surpanakha), who then is hurt when Rama shoots at the deer. Source: Kamil Bulke - Many Ramayanas - Ramakatha. Picture Credit: Ramanujam SR

hook: One of our colonisers first translated Ramayana into a European language. Which one?
explanation: The Portuguese missionary J Fenichio wrote a book ‘Libro da Saita’ in 1609. He wrote about the ten avataras of Vishnu and the story of Rama. He largely follows Valmiki. The existing manuscript covers the account from Dashratha’s yajna to Sita’s agnipariksha. Some pages are lost so we don’t know if wrote more. He composed it in the South India and his account of Sita’s and Hanuman’s birth reflect Southern versions. In the 17th and 18th centuries, the story of Rama was mentioned in many Dutch, French & Spanish works. The story of Rama first appeared in English when Abbe Dubois’ French book was translated into English in the early part of the 19th cent CE. Source: Kamil Bulke - Many Ramayanas - Ramakatha.

hook: Hobutsushu is a Japanese version of Ramayana dated to the 12th Century CE. Hanuman is an incarnation of?
explanation: Hobutsushu is a collection of stories one of which resembles an abridged version of Ramayana. It is believed to have come through the Chinese version of Ramayana, the Tripitaka, and therefore carries Buddhist influence. According to the text, a group of monkeys supported Rama in his search for Sita. Brahma Sakka (or Indra, the ruler of heavens) was impressed by the sacrifice of Rama sincerity of the monkeys. He transformed himself into a small monkey and guided the army to victory. Source: Minoru Hara, Textual theme of Ramayana in Japan Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons, A Hobutsushu book

hook: Ramayana’s journey eastward is well known. It also flowed west. In which language is the 9th century Khotani Ramayana written?
explanation: In about the end of the ninth century we even find an East Iranian version of the Ramayana in Khotanese, an Iranian dialect prevalent in Khotan in Central Asia. The Khotani Ramayana is influenced by the slightly older Tibetan version. The key difference is that in the Khotani Ramayana, Rama is not Vishnu’s avatar but a Bodhisatva. The text starts with a preface in which the Buddha preaches his philosophy and it is made known that Buddha was Rama in a previous life while Maitreya (future Buddha) was Lakshmana. Khotani is termed an East Iranian language and is different from Persian. Kingdom of Khotani is now part of Xinjiang. Source: Lokesh Chandra, Ramayana - The Epic of Asia Kamil Bulke - Many Ramayanas - Ramakatha. Picture Credit: Aurel Stein, Wikimedia Commons. Cave where the Tibetan version of Ramayana was found. Khotani Ramayana is based on Tibetan version.

hook: Who was the scribe for Mahabharata?
explanation: A scribe is someone who used to write down documents. This job was relevant when printing was not invented and any book had to be manually written down. Ganesha was the scribe for Mahabharata as per popular stories. However, in the original Mahabharata this is not the case.

hook: The material Ganesha used to write the Mahabharata is
explanation: Ganesha broke his own tusk to write down the Mahabharata. This is the reason he appears with a broken tusk. This is one of the many stories related to the broken tusk of Ganesha. There are also stories that when moon laughed at Ganesha (because Ganesha fell off Mooshika), Ganasha got upset at the moon. He broke his tusk and threw it at the moon because of which the moon also got blemishes.

hook: What are Koota slokas in Mahabharata?
explanation: Ganesha told Vyasa that he would write the Mahabharata down continuously and Vyasa should not pause and make Ganesha wait. Vyasa in return said “you can only write down what you understand.” Sometimes Vyasa had to stop and think. To make sure that he gets the time, he used to dictate difficult slokas which would take time for Ganesha to understand. Thus Vyasa got some time to think. These slokas are called Koota slokas. There are about 8000 Koota slokas in Mahabharata.

hook: Where is Vyasa Guha located?
explanation: Vyasa Guha is near Badrinath, which is one of the Char Dhams. This is slightly upstream of Badrinath temple. It is located in front of the Saraswati river. The original Saraswati flowed from Himalayas to Arabian Sea. When Vyasa was performing Tapas, the river was making a lot of noise. Vyasa requested Saraswati to be quiet. Even now, you can see that the river is very silent in front of Vyasa Guha. The river Saraswati also joins the river Alaknanda nearby at a place called Keshava Prayaga. The river Alakananda flows through Himalayas and joins the river Bhagirathi at Dev Prayag. Thereafter it is called Ganga.

hook: What was the Arabian sea in ancient times?
What was the bay of bengal known in ancient times?
At which teerth kshetra did river Saraswathi join the sea in ancient times?
What was the special quality Santanu had, which was reflected in his name?
explanation: Pratipa was the King of Hastinapura. The river Ganga wanted to marry Pratipa. He did not agree but he told Ganga that she can marry his son who would be born later. Shantanu was born to Pratipa. He had the capacity to heal anyone with just his touch.

hook: Pratipa was the King of Hastinapura. Later on Pandu also became King of Hastinapura. What was the relationship between Pandu and Pratipa?
explanation: Though Pandu became King of Hastinapura because of his lineage, he was not a blood relation of Pratipa. Shantanu was born to Pratipa and got married to Satyavathi. Their sons Chitrangada and Vichitravirya died without children. So Satyavathi’s son from an earlier marriage, Vyasa had children with Vichitravirya’s wives Ambika and Ambalika. Pandu was born to Ambalika. Thus, Pandu has no direct blood relation with Pratipa.

hook: How are Parashara and Dhritarashtra biologically related?
explanation: When Vichitravirya dies, the kingdom of Hastinapura is left without any son who can become a king. His mother Satyavathi is worried. Satyavathi mentions to Bhishma that she already had a son before she married Shantanu. His name was Krishna Dvaipayana (Vyasa), who was born to Satyavathi and Parashar. She called Krishna Dvaipayana and requested him to have children with Vichitravirya’s wives. When Ambika meets Krishna Dvaipayana she closes her eyes to avoid the radiance of Vyasa. A child was born to them but it was blind since Ambika had closed her eyes. The child is Dhritarashtra. Thus Dhritarashtra is the son of Vyasa who is the son of Parashara, thus becoming the grandson of Parashara.

hook: What was Bhishma's original name?
explanation: Ganga had taken a promise from Shantanu that he will not question anything she does. Ganga would pick up her child as soon as it was born, she would throw it in the river Ganga. This was very painful for Shantanu to bear. But he could not stop his wife. This happened seven times. When the eighth child was born, he stopped Ganga and questioned her. Ganga said since you have broken the promise, I will leave you. In some stories the child is with Shantanu. Deva. In another version of the story, Ganga took the eighth child with her. Afte many years, Shantanu goes near the river Ganga but notices that the river has slowed down. He finds a small boy who had slowed down the river with his arrows. Then Ganga also appears and gives the boy to Shantanu. The boy is Devavratha who came to be called Bhishma later.

hook: What was Dasarajas' condition for Satyavathi to marry Shantanu?
explanation: Shantanu comes across another woman near the river Yamuna. Her name was Satyavathi. He falls in love with Satyavathi but realizes that since he is the father of a grown up son, this is not possible. He returns sadly to the palace. Shantanu’s Son Devaratha noticed the sadness and asked his father what was wrong. Shantanu told him that he is concerned that if Devaratha gets hurt and dies he will be left without a son. An elder tells Devaratha about his father’s love for Satyavathi. Devaratha goes to Dasha Raja, Satyavathi’s father. Dasha Raja puts a condition that the children of Satyavathi should become the king after Shantanu.

hook: When did Devavratha get the name of Bhishma?
explanation: Dasaraja puts a condition that that the children of Satyavathi should become the king after Shantanu. Devavratha agrees that neither himself nor his children will stake a claim for the throne. Thus, the children of Satyavathi and Shantanu can become Kings. Dasha Raja is not convinced that Devavratha’s children will keep the promise. Therefore Devavratha takes a vow that he won’t get married so that there is no chance of his children staking a claim to the throne. Because of the sacrifice that Devavratha made for his father, he is called Bhishma.

hook: Bhishma, Chitrangada and Vichitravirya are related. How?
explanation: They are half brothers. Both Chitrangada and Vichitravirya were born to Shantanu and Satyavathi. Bhishma is born to Shantanu and Ganga. Thus they are half-brothers.

hook: Who became the king after Santhanu?
explanation: After Shantanu dies, Chitrangada becomes a king. He went to the forest for hunting. He came across the king of Gandharvas whose name was also Chitrangada. The king fought with Gandharva saying there can only be one person with the name Chitrangada. The King is defeated and dies. Later on Vichitravirya also dies without children. Thus, Bhishma, Chitrangada and Vichitravirya all do not have any children.

hook: The sons of Ambika and Ambalika were
explanation: Vichitravirya becomes a good king after his brother Chitrangada dies. But he does not find a wife. Bhishma goes to Varanasi since the King of Kashi was performing the swayamwara of his three daughters – Amba, Ambika and Ambalika. Bhishma took the daughters as captives and also defeated all other Kings. The oldest daughter Amba said she is already in love with another person and therefore Bhishma let her go. Ambika and Ambalika were married to Vichitravirya. Vichitravirya also dies before he has children.

hook: Bhishma arranges several marriages. Which marriage does he NOT arrange
explanation: Bhishma arranges the marriage of his father Shantanu. Later on, he also abducts Ambika and Ambalika for marriage to his half brother Vichitravirya. When Dhritarashtra had to be married it was difficult to find a girl since he was blind. Bhishma goes to Gandhara and gave a lot of money to the King to get his daughter married to Dhritarashtra. Gandhari and 11 sisters were married to Dhritarashtra. Along with them Sakuni also came. Gandhari blindfolded herself since her husband did not have eyesight. For Pandu, Bhishma gave a lot of money to the King Madra and got his daughter Madri married to Pandu. Because of the promise he gave to Dasaraja, whose daughter Bhishma’s father was in love with, Bhishma remains unmarried himself.

hook: In the shloka acaturvadano brahmā..., Vyasa is being compared to?
explanation: acaturvadano brahmā dvibāhuraparo harih| abhālalocana śambhur bhagavān bādarāyana || This is the first sloka of Mahabharata. This sloka means Vyasa is like Brahma but without four faces. It also means he is like Vishnu with two hands. It also means Vyasa is like Shiva but does not have the third eye. The last word in the Sloka, “Badarayanah,” is another name for Vyasa. Vyasa is like Brahma, the creator of the world since he created a large MahaKavya 5000 years ago. Vyasa is like Vishnu and sustains the world by educating us about good values. Like Shiva, Vyasa helps us dissolve the negative qualities that we have.

hook: In the sloka itihāsapradīpena..., Mahabharata is being compared to?
explanation: itihāsapradīpena mohāvaranaghātinā| lokagarbhagrham krtsnam yathāvat samprakāśitam|| The lamp of Itihaasa removes the ignorance that we have. The lamp brightens and shows us the essence of the whole world as it is. It shows us the reality as it is, nothing more or less. That is Mahabharata. Thus, the sloka compared Mahabharata to a lamp, that too to the primary lamp (Pradipa) that lights all other lamps.

hook: Who wears Mahabharata and Ramayana as ornaments?
explanation: niśrenyau brahmalokasya vāgdevyāh karnakundale | dharmadrumamahāmūle vālmīkivyāsayoh krtī|| In this Sloka, in the tree of Dharma, the roots are said to be the creations of Valmiki and Vyasa, i.e Ramayana and Mahabharata. These two Kavyas are the staircase to heaven. These two Kavyas are the ornaments of Devi Saraswati.

hook: “Sham no dvipade, sham chatushpade” - Which character broke this principle in Mahabharata?
explanation: Hunting is extremely painful to the environment and shouldn't be done for fun. Our culture preaches – Sham no dvipade, sham chatushpade let there be joy to the creatures walking on two legs, as well as four. Hunting was never supposed to be an act of pleasure in ancient Bharat. It was a practice, done by the Hunters for a living and the Kings to sharpen their war skills. There were specific rules to hunt, so that the natural setting of the animals is not disrupted, hunting was forbidden during the mating seasons. Hunting was only an occasional activity for the Kings and not a regular sport. King Pandu made such a mistake of hunting for fun, once.

hook: Which sage had given Kunti a mantra when she was young?
explanation: When Kunti was just 12 years old, she was blessed by Sage Durvasa as he was impressed by her hospitality. The sage, being far-sighted, gave her a boon, through which she could get a son from any god that she wished to invoke.

hook: Who was the first Son of Kunti?
explanation: Young Kunti received a boon from Durvasa that she could get a son from any god that she wished to invoke. Filled with curiosity, Kunti invoked the Sun God - Suryanarayana soon after receiving the boon. To her astonishment, the Sun god appeared and blessed her with a radiant baby, gifted with divine earrings and armour. Thus, Karna was born. Kunti feared that she would be unable to answer the society, for having a child. She put him in a golden basket with a lot of wealth and floated him off in a river. The charioteer of Bhishma- Adhiratha Sushain, found this child and brought him home. His wife mothered Karna with great affection. Her name was Radha, hence Karna was also called Radhey. Since he was sent with a lot of gold, Karna was called Vashusena.

hook: Yudhishtira is a word made of Yudhi and Shtira. Shtira means stable. Stable in what?
explanation: Yudhishtira Yudhi Sthirah - Yudhi Sthirah - युधि स्थिरः युधिष्ठिरः - Stable in battle Kunti prayed to Yamadharma, the guardian of Dharma for her first child. Yudhishtira was born, the one with great intellect, poise and balance. He would be very stable in war.

hook: Who was the second wife of Pandu?
explanation: Madri too wished to have children, Kunti transferred her boon to Madri, enabling her to invoke the blessings of a god. Madri invoked the Ashvini Kumaras and begot twins, Nakula and Sahadeva. They both were charming and possessed healing powers. They were very skillful in the field of medicine. Sahadeva could foretell the future as well. Soon, after this, Pandu passed away. Once, when he was in the company of Madri, he forgot his curse, and fell dead. Following this, Madri too gave up her life.

hook: Who had adopted Kunti and what was the name of her brother?
Who was the first son of Gandhari and Dhritarashtra?
How many parts did the fetus of Gandhari break into?
explanation: When Kunti gave birth to Yudhishtira, Gandhari was still pregnant. Realising that Kunti had already given birth to the eldest son of the family and as per tradition, he will inherit the throne, Gandhari grew impatient. She forcefully pulled out the fetus. At this disastrous site, Vyasa Maharishi arrived and saved the fetus by smearing the fetus with water. The fetus split into 100 pieces. He put each of the piece in a Kumbha containing ghee. After attaining maturity, the pots broke and from them emerged the 99 sons of Dhritarashtra, the eldest being Duryodhana along with one daughter.

hook: The Kauravas are also known as?
Drupada was Dronacharya’s childhood friend. What did he promise Drona?
explanation: Sage Bharadvaja was a great teacher. Among his disciples were Drona and Drupada. Drona was his own son and Drupada was the crown prince of Panchala. Drona and Drupada were thick friends. Looking at the poverty of Drona, Drupada had promised half of his kingdom to his friend, when he became King in future. Both the friends got busy with life as they grew up.

hook: Whom did Dronacharya marry?
explanation: Dronacharya married Kripi, who is the sister of Kripacharya. Kripacharya was the first Guru of Kauravas and Pandavas.

hook: Which incident was the trigger for Dronacharya becoming the Guru for Pandavas and Kauravas?
explanation: Drona was extremely poor and had no money to even feed his son Ashvatthama properly. His son had never drank milk in his life, his friends would tease him saying he has never seen milk. Saddened by this, Drona mixed wheat flour and sugar in water and fed it to his son, saying that it is milk. Soon, young Ashvatthama realised this folly and felt cheated by his parents, Drona dejected by his terrible condition, approached his old and dear friend Drupada, and reminded him of the promise he had once made. Drupada, now a proud king, insulted and mocked the poverty of Drona and sent him away. Betrayed by his friend, Drona resolved to train capable kshatriyas, who would be better Kings than Drupada and would be able to defeat Drupada in battle. Eventually this would lead to Drona becoming the Guru of Kauravas and Pandavas. While the poverty of Drona made him approach Draupada, it was only the breaking of promise that made Drona take a vow.

hook: When Drona first met the Kuru princes, they were playing an ancient Indian game, that today’s kids still play?
explanation: Veeti-khelna in Sanskrit is colloquially known as gilli-danda. The colloquial name describes the game – use a big stick (danda) to hit a small stick (danda). While video games and modern sports like football & cricket hold the attention of kids today, gilli-danda is still played for fun. When you play gilli danda and people around you complain that it is not glamorous, remind them of the games rich heritage stretching back to the Mahabharata.

hook: While the Kuru princes played veeti-khelna, the small stick fell at a place from where the princes couldn’t get it back. Where did it fall?
explanation: As the Kuru princes played, the gilli fell inside a well. Unable to get the stick out, the princes stood around the well helplessly. Guru Drona, who was on his way to Hastinapur, noticed the princes in this helpless state.

hook: What did Drona use to get the stick out of the well?
explanation: Drona too a blade of glass and threw it forcefully and accurately at the stick. The blade of grass stuck tightly to the stick. Then he threw another blade which stuck to the first blade. He kept on throwing blades which stick to the previous blade and created a rope. He then pulled the stick out easily. He berated the princes asking them what kind of kshatriyas were they if they could not do such a simple task.

hook: What does the root ‘kshata’ in the word kshatriya mean?
explanation: Kshatriya is defined as kshata (wound)-trayate(protect) – someone who protects you from wounds. The modern Hindi word kshati (क्षति) which means ‘damage’ comes from the root kshata.

hook: Drona instructed the Kuru princes to narrate the story of how he had got the stick out of the well to a senior member of the Kuru family. Who was this person?
explanation: Drona wanted to take revenge on his friend Drupada. He was looking for suitable employment as Guru so that he could train a deserving student who would help him get revenge. That’s why, he was travelling to the Kuru capital Hastinapur. Much like, how young people migrate to large cities like Mumbai & Bengaluru from their hometowns in search of high paying work. He instructed the princes to approach Bhishma as he knew that as their grandfather he would indulge them and appoint him as their Guru. Drona knew human psychology – grandparents indulge grandkids more than the parents. This story demonstrates the first instance that shows that though Drona was a great teacher, he was also a selfish person. He wanted to be Guru of the Kuru princes since they were 105 of them and surely, one among so many could help him get revenge.
